story:
The council is putting up a child care center on the plot beside our house—the very ground where Mr. Muleya once lived. He was a stout, bald pensioner, a practical man with a fervent belief in the nobility of solitude. For twelve years he perfected the art of being alone, always finding people too something—too loud, too nosy, too forward, too provincial. In his eyes, society was irredeemably inadequate; his own standards hovered above everyone.

His philosophy had a way of showing up in brick and wire. He posted a warning about dogs, even though he never owned any, and he loathed our dogs enough to make them the chief targets of his resentment. The feud turned ugly—he allegedly tried to poison them—until we stepped in and set a firm rule: our dogs would never cross onto his property. He insisted he was reasonable. The dogs, he maintained, had no sense of boundaries; they fouled his lawn, dug along the boundary wall, and trampled his garden. He cataloged their crimes endlessly.

He set about building his masterpiece of seclusion: a very high wall, the final component of a private fortress that would keep out dogs, neighbors, and the wandering eyes and feet of neighborhood children. He had planned for razor wire for the top, an electric line to "encourage good behavior," and a security notice that would remind trespassers of the wisdom of compliance. The foundation was laid, pillars positioned, but the wall was still under construction—a future architectural diagram of his desire to stay aloof.

One afternoon, when our boys and their cousins were on school holiday and happily busy outdoors, his voice shattered the peace. He had a grating squeak that made even ordinary words sound like an accusation. We ran over and found the boys stricken with fear. Their ball had sailed into his yard. With the wall still incomplete and only waist-high sections standing, they had easily stepped over the low barrier and onto fresh concrete cast for the wall's pillars and left tiny footprints, each one a small rebellion stamped into the fortress façade. The marks were shallow but undeniable. I nearly laughed at the unintended effect: what was meant to become an intimidating fortress looked, with those small footprints, frankly like something that belonged at a child care center. I didn't laugh. His fury was real. If he was allergic to people in general, he was especially allergic to little ones.

Murphy's Law caught up with him on a quiet day. I sat at my usual place, deep in an essay on the paradoxes of human existence, so absorbed I missed the first signs. Passersby noticed the smoke and then his cries. Flames rose with a suddenness that felt provoked by his own panicked squeaks. We tried to help, but his house had been designed to keep people out. The intercom worked; the doors did not. He died clawing at the automatic door that sealed itself against him. The police later tore the doors down to retrieve him—his body blackened, severely burned by the home he'd fortified.

Now, where solitude once built a castle, the council is raising a child care center. The ground has changed its mind. Where wire, walls, and warnings once stood, there will be small voices, open gates, and footprints welcome in the concrete.
